html_content:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola's revenue decline of 9.9% in FY25 (Rs 5,010 Cr → Rs 4,514 Cr) masks a far deeper collapse: quarterly revenue fell 71% from peak (Rs 1,644 Cr in Q1 FY25) to trough (Rs 470 Cr in Q4 FY26) — then partially recovered to Rs 520 Cr. The full-year decline was cushioned by H1 FY25 being strong before the Risks/Service_Center_Crisis fully hit. But the underlying trend is brutal: monthly sales dropped from 53,320 units (Mar-24) to 7,567 (Nov-25) — an 85.8% decline. The decline is a direct consequence of the trust crisis, not market contraction — the total E2W market grew from 119K to 149K monthly units over the same period.

Revenue Decline FY25

Criticality: 🔴 HIGH

---

The Reversal

After explosive growth from FY22-FY24 (Rs 373 Cr → Rs 5,010 Cr), Companies/Ola Electric's revenue declined 9.9% in FY25 to Rs 4,514 Cr. The quarterly decline was far steeper.

---

What Changed

External

  • FAME subsidy reduction (40%→15% cap) increased vehicle prices by Rs 22,784-Rs 37,106
  • Competition intensified as incumbents scaled EV offerings — Companies/TVS Motor reached 23% share, Companies/Bajaj Auto hit 21%, Companies/Ather Energy grew 75% YoY
  • Market/PM E-DRIVE Subsidy subsidy ends Jul 2026 — creating additional price risk for H2 FY27

Internal

  • Quality concerns accumulated, affecting repeat purchases and word-of-mouth
  • Service gaps created friction in customer experience
  • Brand Perception shifted from "Exciting" to "Unreliable"
  • The Risks/CCPA_Regulatory_Intervention show-cause notice (Oct 2024) triggered negative media coverage across ET, Mint, Business Standard

---

Quarterly Sales Decline

| Quarter | Revenue (₹ Cr) | Units Sold | Blended ASP | Gross Margin |
|---------|---------------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Mar-24 (peak) | 1,598 | — | — | — |
| Jun-24 | 1,644 | 108,000 | Rs 1,35,000 | 18% |
| Sep-24 | 1,214 | 95,000 | Rs 1,22,000 | 16% |
| Dec-24 | 1,045 | 82,000 | Rs 1,10,000 | 14% |
| Mar-25 | 611 | 74,000 | Rs 98,000 | 12% |
| Jun-25 | 828 | 48,000 | Rs 95,000 | 22% |
| Sep-25 | 690 | 32,000 | Rs 92,000 | 28% |
| Dec-25 | 470 | 35,000 | Rs 89,000 | 34.3% |
| Mar-26 | 520 | 39,000 | Rs 90,000 | 36% |

From peak (Jun-24) to trough (Dec-25): -71% decline in quarterly revenue. Units collapsed 70% (108K → 32K). But gross margins tripled from trough (12% → 36%).

---

Implications

1. Cash burn accelerates — lower revenue + fixed costs = larger losses (FY25 loss: Rs 2,276 Cr, worst ever)
2. Market share loss — revenue declining while market growing: went from 44.69% to 15.95% share
3. Financing dependence — FY25 financing cash flow +Rs 5,429 Cr to stay afloat; cash at Rs 3,516 Cr (Mar-25)
4. Valuation erosion — stock at Rs 36.1 (80.7% below post-IPO peak ~Rs 150), market cap Rs 15,923 Cr
5. Financial/Margin_Paradox — despite revenue decline, the company is restructuring into viability at lower volumes

---

---

Data Sources

DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
Quarterly revenue trajectory: Rs 1,644 Cr (Q1 FY25) → Rs 1,214 Cr (Q2 FY25) → Rs 1,100 Cr (Q3 FY25) → Rs 611 Cr (Q4 FY25) → Rs 828 Cr (Q1 FY26) → Rs 690 Cr (Q2 FY26) → Rs 470 Cr (Q3 FY26) → Rs 520 Cr (Q4 FY26). The 71% peak-to-trough decline happened in 6 quarters. Units sold: 108K → 32K (trough) → 39K (latest).
DATA: Source: PRODUCT_MIX_QUARTERLY.csv
The revenue decline was amplified by a product mix shift to cheaper models. The Opportunities/S1 X (entry-level, ~Rs 70K) grew from 15% to 72% of sales, collapsing blended ASP from Rs 1,35,000 to Rs 90,000 (-33%). S1 Pro fell from 35% to 7%, S1 Air from 10% to 5%. However, the story has a positive side: gross margin doubled from 18% to 36% because Technology/Gigafactory_Updates in-house cells cut battery cost from $130 to $85/kWh, making even cheaper scooters more profitable per unit.
DATA: Source: E2W_MONTHLY_REGISTRATIONS.csv
The monthly VAHAN data shows the precise mechanics of the decline. Ola registrations: 53,320 (Mar-24) → 33,963 (Apr-24, -36% in one month post-FAME expiry) → 31,000 → 30,000 → 29,000 → 28,000 → 27,000 → 26,000 (Oct-24, CCPA month) → continuing decline to 7,567 (Nov-25). YoY growth went from +146.98% to -69.73%. The worst of the decline occurred while the total market grew from ~84,000 to ~149,000 — Ola lost share in a rapidly growing market.
DATA: Source: QUARTERLY_FINANCIALS_EXT.csv
The EBITDA turnaround is the company's best-kept secret: from -22.1% (Q4 FY25, loss ~Rs 243 Cr) to +4.5% (Q4 FY26, profit ~Rs 23 Cr) — a 26.6 percentage point swing in 4 quarters. Management focus shifted from "Crisis Management & Hyperservice" to "Structural Profitability & LFP Pivot." OpEx was slashed from Rs 840 Cr to Rs 484 Cr (42% reduction in 2 quarters).
DATA: Source: ola/QUATERS.csv
Ola's quarterly revenue collapsed from Rs 1,644 Cr (Q1 FY25) to Rs 470 Cr (Q3 FY26) - a 71% decline in 7 quarters. Meanwhile, unit sales dropped from 108,000 to 35,000 per quarter (68% decline), driven by the service crisis and trust erosion.
Causal chain: The service crisis (CCPA notice Oct-2024, 10,000+ complaints, 7-15 day turnarounds) directly caused customers to switch to Ather/TVS/Bajaj, triggering a revenue death spiral
Cells: Row 2 Col Jun-24 = 1644, Row 2 Col Dec-25 = 470; PRODUCT_MIX Row 2 Units_Sold = 108000, Row 8 Units_Sold = 35000
DATA: Source: ola/PRODUCT_MIX_QUARTERLY.csv
Ola's sales trajectory shows stabilization in Q4 FY26 (39,000 units, Rs 520 Cr) compared to Q2 FY26 trough (32,000 units, Rs 450 Cr). The Gen 3 S1 launch and service improvements are showing early signs of volume recovery, but at 39K/quarter vs 108K/quarter peak.
Causal chain: Q4 FY26 stabilization (39K units) suggests Ola may have found a floor, but at 36% of peak quarterly volume (108K), the company needs to nearly 3x current sales to return to FY25-level revenue
Cells: Row 7 (Q2_FY26): Units=32000, Revenue=450; Row 9 (Q4_FY26): Units=39000, Revenue=520; Row 2 (Q1_FY25): Units=108000, Revenue=1644
DATA: Source: E2W_MONTHLY_REGISTRATIONS.csv
Ola's YoY growth turned negative for the first time in Aug-24 at -3.45%, then worsened progressively to -69.73% by Nov-25 - the worst single-month decline in the dataset. The negative growth persisted for 17 consecutive months (Aug-24 to Dec-25) before turning slightly positive again at +1.19% in Mar-26.
Causal chain: Service issues compound -> negative growth accelerates -> brand enters death spiral -> extreme OpEx cuts -> fragile stabilization
Cells: Row 30 Ola_YoY_Growth_Pct=-3.45; Row 45 Ola_YoY_Growth_Pct=-69.73; Row 49 Ola_YoY_Growth_Pct=1.19
DATA: Source: PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Ola Electric's annual revenue grew from Rs 0.86 Cr in FY21 to Rs 5,009.83 Cr in FY24 (5,826x growth over 3 years), then declined to Rs 4,514 Cr in FY25 (-9.9% YoY decline) as the service crisis hit sales.
Causal chain: Massive scale-up post-IPO was followed by service quality failures that triggered demand collapse and revenue decline in FY25.
Cells: Row 2: Sales values for Mar-21 through Mar-25
DATA: Source: PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Raw Material Cost escalated from Rs 641.12 Cr (FY22) to Rs 4,460.66 Cr (FY24), then declined to Rs 3,699 Cr (FY25) as sales volume dropped. Change in Inventory flipped from positive Rs 81.14 Cr (FY24) to negative Rs -9 Cr (FY25), indicating destocking.
Causal chain: Sales decline forced destocking as demand fell faster than production could be adjusted.
Cells: Row 3: Raw Material Cost, Row 4: Change in Inventory for Mar-22 through Mar-25
DATA: Source: financial_mda.txt
Total 247,437 units of Ola S1 and S1 Pro sold from inception through Jun 30, 2023. Scooter sales volumes: 20,948 units (FY22), 156,251 units (FY23), 70,238 units (Q1 FY24 alone).
Causal chain: From 0 to 247K units in 18 months demonstrated rapid execution capability that impressed IPO investors.
Cells: Page 314-315: Sales volume data
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Pre-IPO risk: Ola derives revenue "solely from the sale of limited electric vehicle scooter models." As of October 31, 2023, only 4 models delivered (Ola S1 Pro Dec 2021, Ola S1 Sep 2022, Ola S1 Air Aug 2023, Ola S1 X+ Dec 2023). If a particular scooter model is "not well-received by the market," sales and financial condition could be materially affected.
Context: Page 40, Risk Factor 12
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Pre-IPO order cancellation data: 6-9% of fully paid confirmed orders cancelled (Oct 2022-May 2023). FAME subsidy cut caused spike to 24% cancellation rate in June 2023. Pre-order deposits: ₹499 (S1 Pro, S1), ₹999 (S1 Air, S1 X+, S1 X models).
Context: Page 43-44, Risk Factor 18
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Demand estimation risk: Ola has "limited insights into consumer trends" as a new entrant with limited operating history. Lead time of 3-6 months from purchase order to delivery. If demand overestimated: excess inventory and storage costs. If underestimated: production delays and revenue loss.
Context: Page 36-37, Risk Factor 6
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Capacity utilization risk: total licensed installed capacity of 1M units as of Oct 31, 2023, but only 36% utilization (FY23) and 17% (FY22). Low demand or low capacity utilization limits ability to leverage economies of scale, impacting margins.
Context: Page 37, Risk Factor 6
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Seasonality of 2W demand: demand generally peaks Jan-Mar and during festive season (Sep-Nov), declines in December. FAME subsidy cut (June 2023) immediately caused orders to decline 58.14%. Ola's limited history makes it difficult to judge exact seasonality patterns.
Context: Page 55, Risk Factor 44
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Advertising spend trend: ₹15.95M (FY21), ₹494.20M (FY22), ₹614.70M (FY23), ₹131.18M (Q1 FY24). As % of revenue: 184.61% (FY21), 13.23% (FY22), 2.34% (FY23), 1.06% (Q1 FY24). Ola relied on brand strength and free channels (Ola Cabs app push, word of mouth) rather than traditional media.
Context: Page 39, Risk Factor 11
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Pre-IPO risk: "if our charging infrastructure is not conveniently accessible to customers or if our aftersales services...are not efficient, our brand name and reputation could be negatively impacted, thus leading to slower growth in new customers."
Context: Page 39, Risk Factor 11

Connections

### Causes

- Trust crisis: Risks/CCPA_Regulatory_Intervention notice (Oct 2024) and 10,000+ complaints destroyed buyer confidence

- Service network failure: 7-15 day repair backlogs vs legacy 1-2 days pushed customers to TVS/Bajaj/Ather

- Subsidy reduction: FAME II to EMPS (Rs 40K → Rs 5K per vehicle) forced ASP cuts of Rs 37,000

- Competition: TVS/Bajaj/Hero absorbed every unit Ola lost — combined legacy share at 65.23%

- Product mix cannibalization: S1 X at 72% of sales collapsed blended ASP from Rs 1.35L to Rs 0.90L

### Effects

- Larger losses: FY25 net loss Rs 2,276 Cr — 44% worse than FY24

- Cash burn spike: Operating cash outflow Rs 2,391 Cr in FY25 (nearly 4x FY24)

- Stock collapse: ~80.7% from post-IPO peak

- Reduced investment capacity: "Deep Negative" EBITDA prevents service network expansion or R&D

- Recovery path emerges: EBITDA positive (+4.5%) for first time in Q4 FY26 — the restructuring is working

### Amplifiers

- Negative operating leverage: 70% volume decline spread fixed costs (Rs 566 Cr depreciation, Rs 366 Cr interest) over fewer units

- PM E-DRIVE subsidy cliff: Jul 2026 — Rs 5,000 subsidy removal equals 123% of current per-unit profit

- Market/Google Play Store Rating rating gap (3.2 vs competitors 4.0-4.2) erodes D2C conversion at every research touchpoint

- Negative NPS turns existing owners into anti-salespeople

### Linked Nodes

- Financial/Revenue Trajectory

- Financial/Quarterly Losses

- Financial/Cash Flow Analysis

- Risks/Financial Losses — Net loss worsening as revenue declines

- Risks/Service_Center_Crisis — Direct cause of demand destruction

- Risks/Customer_Sentiment_Analysis — Trust deficit drove purchase deterrence

- Risks/Subsidy Dependency — FAME removal forced price cuts

- Risks/Competition — Legacies captured lost share

- Opportunities/Mass Market E2W — S1 X as recovery play

- Opportunities/Motorcycle Market — New TAM for growth

- Financial/Margin_Paradox — Revenue down, margins up story
